metadata.title: The Instant a Nanoparticle Hits Blood, It Vanishes Under a Coat of Protein
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut — Candidate 08. Source chapter: cancer-nanomedicine/chapters/04-targeting-epr-protein-corona-and-active-ligands.md (protein corona masking mechanism only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on newsprint; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, teal = engineered particle surface and targeting ligands, crimson = protein corona and masking and liver clearance, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = engineered particle surface / targeting ligands; CRIMSON = protein corona / masking / liver clearance; GOLD = the moment of corona formation
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: passive vs active targeting mechanics (own video), the EPR debate, engineer-the-corona angle, patient-to-patient variability. One image: proteins swarm and bury the ligand.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Pragmatist register (voices/pragmatist/VOICE.md + HAI.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the HAI outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Humanitarians AI
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Months of engineering a nanoparticle's surface — antibodies loaded to lock onto cancer cells. It enters blood. Within seconds, the surface is buried.

card.copy: The Instant a Nanoparticle Hits Blood, It Vanishes Under a Coat of Protein
card.sub: why targeting that works in culture fails in blood

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] months of engineering — buried in seconds

beats.narration_text: A nanoparticle decorated with targeting antibodies binds cancer cells in cell culture. Inject the same particle in blood: it accumulates in the liver. The antibodies are still attached. What changed?

card.copy: The antibodies are still attached. Why does the particle end up in the liver?

beats.narration_text: On entry into blood, plasma proteins adsorb onto the particle surface immediately. Albumin arrives first, then immunoglobulins, fibrinogen, apolipoproteins. A dense protein coat forms within seconds.

props.headline: The moment a nanoparticle enters blood, plasma proteins begin adsorbing onto…

beats.scene_description: a nanoparticle surrounded by a swarm of protein molecules — albumin, globulins — in a blood plasma environment; proteins piling on a teal spherical particle, editorial diagram style on aged newsprint
beats.image_prompt: editorial diagram of a spherical nanoparticle being surrounded by plasma proteins in blood — albumin (large oval shapes), immunoglobulins (Y-shaped), fibrinogen — swarming and adhering to the particle surface, flat print reproduction on aged newsprint, desaturated palette with teal particle and crimson proteins, schematic scientific illustration
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL] proteins swarm the particle in blood

beats.narration_text: This is the protein corona. Albumin, immunoglobulins, complement fragments, opsonins — adsorbed onto the engineered surface within seconds. The corona is not the surface you designed. It is the surface the body now sees.

production_viz.label: protein corona accumulates on particle
production_viz.mechanic: a teal circle (nanoparticle) with small teal dots on its perimeter (targeting ligands); crimson protein shapes appear one by one and pile on the surface until the teal dots are buried beneath the crimson coat; gold label 'protein corona forms within seconds'
production_viz.note: the core visual of the reel — accumulate manim move. Ligands visible first, then buried.

beats.narration_text: First consequence: the corona physically masks targeting ligands. Proteins adsorbing onto the surface cover the engineered antibodies. The ligand is still attached but cannot contact its receptor — a plasma protein layer blocks access.

production_viz.label: targeting ligand buried under corona — cannot reach receptor
production_viz.mechanic: particle with one teal ligand arm extended; a crimson protein block slides in and covers the ligand; a receptor on a cell surface sits unreachable; annotation label 'ligand masked — cannot bind'
production_viz.note: the masking mechanism — ligand is there but physically blocked

beats.narration_text: Second consequence: opsonins and complement fragments in the corona are immune recognition signals that flag the particle for macrophage uptake in the liver and spleen. The body processes it as a pathogen, not a drug delivery vehicle.

production_viz.label: opsonins on corona flag particle for liver clearance
production_viz.mechanic: the coated particle (full crimson corona) with a CRIMSON arrow pointing to a macrophage shape labeled 'liver'; LabelChip 'opsonin' on the corona; serif label 'flagged for clearance'
production_viz.note: opsonin flag + clearance arrow — the immune redirection

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] opsonin flags particle for liver phagocytosis

beats.narration_text: Cell culture medium contains no plasma proteins — the ligand is unobstructed and binds. Blood contains thousands of proteins. The corona forms, the ligand is buried, and the biodistribution result reverses. These are not the same experimental environment.

production_viz.label: culture medium vs blood — two different worlds
production_viz.mechanic: two side-by-side panels: left 'culture medium' shows a clean teal particle with ligand binding a teal receptor; right 'blood' shows the same particle now coated crimson with ligand buried, arrow to liver; gold dividing line between panels
production_viz.note: the environmental gap — two panels, before and after corona

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two-panel: culture (ligand works) vs blood (ligand buried)

beats.narration_text: Targeting validated only in protein-free culture is incomplete. The bare ligand functions in medium because there is no corona. Test in full plasma — at minimum — before drawing conclusions about in vivo behavior.

card.copy: Testing in culture medium is not testing in blood.
card.sub: the corona is not optional — it is the experimental condition

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] validation requirement: test in full plasma

beats.narration_text: Specific case. A folate-targeted nanoparticle binds folate-receptor-positive cancer cells in culture at 87 percent efficiency. Injected in a mouse with full serum: 72 percent reaches the liver at four hours, 3 percent reaches the tumor. Folate ligands are still attached — buried under a 30-nanometer protein corona that blocks receptor contact. Illustrative numbers.

production_viz.label: folate-targeted particle: 87% in culture, 3% at tumor in blood
production_viz.mechanic: two columns: 'IN CULTURE' column shows teal bar 87% binding; 'IN BLOOD' column shows crimson bar 72% liver, teal bar 3% tumor; serif label 'illustrative numbers' beneath; annotation 'ligands buried' with crimson arrow
production_viz.note: worked example with illustrative numbers as specified in card — always labeled illustrative

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] culture vs blood biodistribution — folate example

beats.narration_text: The protein corona is why targeting that works in a dish fails in a body. Months of surface engineering, neutralized in seconds. The ligand is intact. The corona is in the way. The liver processes exactly what it was signaled to process.
beats.scene_description: end-state of corona graphic — teal particle fully coated in crimson; a HandRing around the corona layer; annotation 'months of engineering' pointing to the buried teal dots; 'seconds of corona' pointing to the crimson layer

production_viz.mechanic: the fully-coated particle; HandRing lands around the crimson corona layer; two serif labels: 'targeting ligands — buried' (teal) and 'protein corona — seconds' (crimson)
production_viz.note: one ring only — the editor's pen on the corona layer. Recap the two labels.

annotation.target: the crimson corona layer around the teal particle

beats.new_visual_element: [ANNOTATE] ring around corona — months vs seconds

beats.narration_text: The corona forms. The ligand is buried. The body never encounters the surface you built.

card.copy: The protein corona buries the targeting ligand. The body sees the corona, not the particle you designed.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: This is part of the Cancer Nanomedicine series from Humanitarians AI.

props.tagline: AI education for humanitarian and social-impact practitioners.
